explanation.text: Sylvie changes the event's name to “Paint Out” and calls it “stellar” because she wants the event to sound exciting and special. In this sentence, “stellar” means amazing.
Sylvie says they should do something special for animals and proposes donating the painting money to an animal shelter. These ideas show that she cares about animals.
Sylvie says it is getting late even though it is only 6:15 in the morning. Sammy's confused face shows that he is surprised she thinks such an early time is late.

Paragraph 11 lists the work Sylvie and Sammy complete before the event: getting permission, contacting the shelter, and inviting people. It demonstrates the saying because this preparation helps make their Paint Out successful.
The students plan to sell paintings and donate the money to the animal shelter. This detail expresses the central idea because their project uses art to make a difference for animals.
Many people arrive, canvases fill with colorful pet pictures, and the Paint Out is soon in full swing. Because the event works so well, Sylvie most likely feels proud of its success.
Paragraph 2 describes a raccoon's body, face mask, paws, and tail. The graphic supports those details because it lets readers see what a raccoon looks like.
Paragraph 7 says raccoons hide during the day to stay safe from predators and people. This explains why they are active and look for food mostly at night.
The “Many Different Dens” section explains that raccoons spend much of winter in their dens but may leave to find food. Therefore, that section contains the winter information.
The passage says exactly what a raccoon eats depends on where it lives. This directly connects habitat to behavior because the available surroundings affect the animal's food choices.
Calling raccoons “very smart and quick” gives the author's positive judgment of them. This detail best shows the author's view because the other choices simply report behaviors or risks.
Marta's legs hurry toward the cookhouse and knock over her table. This action causes the problem because her homework papers then blow out the window.
The wind carries Marta's papers into many places across the village. In this context, “spread” means scattered around.
Marta's homework has been ruined and her glasses have broken, and she thinks of both losses as she fills with grief. These problems explain why she begins to cry.
Grandmother says that when people have a problem, they must choose what they will be. This statement best tells her belief because the rest of her lesson explains different ways to face problems.
After hearing the healing song and story, Marta decides to face her problem like the eagle. This shows that Grandmother's song helps her make a choice.
Marta's decision to be like an eagle shows the lesson she learns about viewing her problem differently and then trying again. This belongs in a summary because it is the story's main resolution.
